reason:
Both answers hit the core substantive points well: push back on unlimited liability with a fees-based cap, keep IP indemnity but narrow its scope with carve-outs and a remedy ladder, don't walk over negotiable boilerplate, and frame the liability cap as the existential asymmetry issue. Both also add reasonable extras (mutual consequential damages waiver, insurance, lawyer review, procurement framing).

The differentiator is execution quality. Answer A is written in broken, telegraphic English throughout ("Enterprise expect redlines. Silence look weak, not agreeable." / "This standard ask; procurement see it thousand times.") — it's grammatically degraded in a way that would look unprofessional and is harder to parse quickly, which matters for a "busy, smart asker." Answer B conveys essentially the same substance (and adds one useful point A lacks: don't agree to indemnify the customer's downstream customers) in clean, well-organized prose with a clearer walk-away condition. Neither has factual/reasoning errors, so this comes down to clarity and usefulness of presentation, where B is clearly better.
